title: The College Degree Is Dying. The CPA Might Be Next. And I Have the Receipts.
excerpt: I am going to say something that is going to upset a lot of accountants and a lot of college administrators at the same time.
body:
I am going to say something that is going to upset a lot of accountants and a lot of college administrators at the same time.

College was not worth it for me. And the CPA might be the next credential to face the same reckoning.

Let me explain both.

I graduated with a B.S.B.A. in Accounting and Finance. I studied hard. I earned a 3.32 GPA. I did everything right by the traditional playbook.

And when I entered the workforce the playbook did not work the way anyone told me it would.

Nobody handed me a career because I had a degree. Nobody opened a door because I sat through four years of coursework. The degree gave me foundational knowledge and I am grateful for that. But as a career accelerant it was one of the most expensive and time consuming paths I could have taken relative to what it actually produced.

What changed my trajectory was not the degree. It was the licenses I earned on my own. The credentials I pursued independently. The projects I completed through Parker Dewey where United Talent Agency recognized me as a top 1% applicant based on what I had built not where I had studied.

The market did not care about the degree the way I was told it would.

Now let us talk about the CPA.

The CPA is alive because the SEC has not allowed anything else to replace it for public company audits. That is the entire foundation the credential is standing on right now. One regulatory requirement. One line in securities law that says a licensed CPA must sign off on public financial statements.

Strip that requirement away and ask yourself honestly how much of what a CPA does could be done by someone with an Enrolled Agent designation, a Series 65, deep QuickBooks proficiency, and two years of real world financial work.

Most of it.

The SEC knows this. The market knows this. But changing audit requirements for public companies is a slow moving regulatory process with enormous institutional resistance from the accounting profession itself. So the CPA remains protected not by its superiority but by its monopoly on a single regulatory function.

That is not a credential. That is a moat built by lobbying.

And the process of getting there? I looked into it. The New Jersey CPA board required me to get a document notarized as part of the application process.

I am a licensed notary public. I notarize documents for a living.

The irony was not lost on me. A credential designed to prove financial sophistication whose entry process involves mailing a notarized form like it is 1987. While I am sitting here with an active NNA certification, FINRA licenses, and clients who text me for same day signings.

That moment told me everything I needed to know about where the CPA sits in 2026.

The Enrolled Agent designation is granted by the IRS itself. It is the highest credential the IRS issues to tax professionals. An EA can represent clients before the IRS in any matter without a CPA license. No state board. No 150 credit hours. No two year work requirement under a licensed supervisor.

The Series 65 licenses you as an Investment Adviser Representative covering portfolio management, financial planning, economics, and ethics at a level most CPA programs barely touch.

Neither requires $150,000 in tuition. Neither takes five to seven years. Neither requires you to get a document notarized before you can even begin.

The accounting industry has done an exceptional job conflating the SEC audit monopoly with general financial competence. They are not the same thing. One is a narrow regulatory function performed by a small percentage of CPAs at public accounting firms. The other is the broad universe of financial work that the vast majority of businesses and individuals actually need.

The college degree and the CPA followed the same trajectory.

Both were sold as the only path. Both were backed by institutional gatekeeping. Both are now being quietly bypassed by people who figured out the alternatives.

The college degree lost its monopoly when remote work dissolved geography and companies started caring more about what you could do than where you studied.

The CPA will lose its monopoly when enough of the market realizes that the SEC audit requirement is a narrow carve out not a broad endorsement of the credential for all financial work.

I am pursuing my Enrolled Agent designation and Series 66. Not because I could not pursue a CPA. But because for the work I am actually doing those credentials are more relevant, more targeted, and more efficient to obtain.

I have active FINRA licenses. QuickBooks ProAdvisor Gold. And completed corporate projects for Reynolds American and United Talent Agency through Parker Dewey.

Nobody has asked me if I am a CPA. Nobody has asked me where I went to school.

They looked at what I built and what I could do.

The market is telling you something. The question is whether you are listening or defending the credentials you already paid for.

The CPA is not dead. The degree is not worthless.

But neither is the only path anymore.

And the people who figure that out early are the ones who end up ahead.

What credential or degree are you questioning right now?

Matthew Coleman is the owner of MCC Solutions LLC, a licensed financial services firm in Cranford NJ. He holds active FINRA SIE and Series 30 licenses, QuickBooks ProAdvisor Gold certification, and is pursuing his Enrolled Agent designation and Series 66.
